data.title: Coca-Cola: The Retirement Dream
data.description:
There are very few companies that carry as little risk as Coca-Cola (KO). Irreplicable distribution and supply chains, unparalleled brand value, and a capital-light business model that's near perfection.

For the retired investors, I argue that buying shares in Coca-Cola should be as pleasant as the sound a Coke can makes when you open it.

The only caveat, as I detailed in my previous "Buy" article, is you better not get too greedy about the potential upside.

The Coca-Cola Investment Thesis

Every investment thesis has three legs: growth, margins, and valuation. With a mature company like Coca-Cola, it's pretty easy to settle on the right expectations for the first two.

On an organic basis, Coke is expected to grow by about 4%-6% annually, driven by volumes and pricing.

With volume, for a company that's already everywhere and anywhere, the key growth driver is population growth, with a little sprinkle of market share gains and further market penetration. On the pricing part, Coke was much smarter than most of its peers, and instead of doing aggressive price hikes, it made incremental changes. Going forward, price hikes are likely to go hand in hand with inflation.

In terms of margins, Coca-Cola has showcased consistent improvements over the years, enabled by its capital-light business model. While there are bumps here and there, I expect to see continued gradual improvement on that front as well.

Together, this adds up to roughly mid-single-digit organic growth and high-single-digit EPS growth. There are your first two legs. We'll talk about the third one, valuation, later in the article.

Fourth Quarter Was Right In Line With The Long-Term Formula

Almost every time you think Coca-Cola is going to surprise you as an investor, chances are, it won't. Like clockwork, Coke delivers beat after beat, exceeding estimates for more than 20 quarters in a row. Last quarter was no exception.

Volumes were up 1%, led by 2% growth in EMEA and LATAM and 1% growth in North America. They saw flat volumes in Asia Pacific and a 6% drop in Bottling Investments, which was largely driven by refranchising certain bottling operations (non-organic). The 1% volume increase compares very favorably to PepsiCo (PEP), which saw a 4% drop in North America.

On the margin front, Coke delivered another quarter of expansion on a comparable basis, with organic operating income growing 13% Y/Y, more than twice the rate of organic revenues, and organic EPS growing 11% Y/Y.

Looking at the FY26 outlook, management expects to deliver another year of right-on-point results, with organic revenue growth of 4%-5% and comparable EPS growth of 7%-8%, as well as strong cash conversion, yielding $12.2 billion in free cash flow.

Valuation Attractive For Entry

So, we know what Coke's long-term trajectory is, and we can see that we can rely on consistent execution from quarter to quarter. The question then becomes, how much should we pay for a high-single-digit EPS growth story with low risk and without much upside surprise potential?

At $77 per share, the market is saying it is willing to pay about 24 times forward earnings, or 22.5 times next year's estimates. On a free cash flow basis, Coke is trading at around 27 times its forward outlook.

This places Coke right in line with its five-year average valuation and a few ticks above the market-average multiple. It also places the stock around usual levels for starting dividend and buyback yields:

So, what we are looking at is a mature company at its normal valuation. To me, for a company like Coke, this is also the fair valuation, which means investors should expect roughly the same level of returns going forward as we saw in the past decade. In other words, about 8%-9% annual returns.

So, Why Is This The Retired Investor's Dream?

Over the past decade, Coke significantly underperformed the market. If we're looking at similar levels of expected returns, the question that comes to mind is, why would an investor sign up for underperformance?

To me, the answer to this question is clear: not all returns are created equal. Coca-Cola experiences far smaller drawdowns and has far lower volatility relative to the market. It increased its dividend payout for 63 consecutive years, with an average increase that's more than double the inflation rate. The disruption risk for Coke is essentially non-existent, and its quarter-to-quarter results are extremely predictable.

In short, the retired investor is getting a "complete package" of very decent reward per unit of risk.

In my previous article covering the company, I laid out the importance of setting your expectations straight when it comes to investing in Coca-Cola.

In short, for the up-and-coming investor who is looking to beat the market and has multiple decades of risk-taking ahead, Coke is, in my mind, irrelevant.

However, for a retired investor who's looking for stable income, capital preservation, and inflation protection, I can't think of a better deal than Coke.

Conclusion

With a starting yield of 2.8% and an earnings growth trajectory of 7%-9%, I estimate Coca-Cola's projected annual returns at 8%-10%, in line with the historical market average.

I still find the current valuation to be a decent entry point for low-risk investors and reiterate the stock at a "Buy."

data.title: Buy HF Sinclair On Bumper Profits
data.description:
I recently completed a review of US oil refiners, and HF Sinclair Corporation (DINO) stood out among its peers with relatively low valuation ratios, high net margin, and low leverage. The company has generated over 60% total returns since I recommended investors continue to hold its stock in early 2023. More recently, the conflict in Iran has disrupted energy markets, resulting in physical shortages of crude oil and fuels in some parts of the world. In concert with measurable impacts, shortage fears and speculation have driven crude oil up almost 50% since late February.

Paradoxically, refiners often benefit from higher crude oil prices, as the price of finished fuels generally follows crude higher. Unlike other industries where the cost of an input commodity will most often reduce profits, finished fuel prices usually keep pace or even outpace crude oil costs such that refining margins increase on expanding crack spread.

Crack Spreads

Refining profits are largely dependent on crack spread, the difference between the cost of crude oil on the input side of a refiner's catalytic cracker and the price of gasoline and diesel on the output side of the cracker. A simplified approach is the 3-2-1 crack spread model with input of one barrel of oil and output of 2/3 barrels of gasoline and 1/3 barrel of diesel. The following plot is based on WTI crude and NY Harbor gasoline and diesel spot prices.

The 3-2-1 crack spread began the year at $22.31/barrel before peaking at over $50/barrel in late March. More recently, the crack spread has moderated to just over $40/barrel, equating to about $0.95 per gallon of fuel.

Dino Performance vs 3-2-1 Crack Spread

Although DINO's operations also include renewables, marketing, lubricants and midstream, its refining segment contributed almost 90% of total YE25 revenue. The company's refining revenues are directly dependent on crack spread.

The Russian invasion of Ukraine in early 2022 may provide some insights and even inform expectations regarding the current conflict centered on Iran. Over the last five years, DINO share prices and trailing 12-month revenue are plotted above the 3-2-1 crack spread and the crack spread's 12-month simple moving average.

Following the Russian invasion in early 2022, DINO share prices climbed as crack spreads expanded from around $20 to over $70/barrel. Later that same year, crack spreads moderated but continued to oscillate between $30 and $45/barrel. With the additional weight of planned refinery maintenance, share prices followed crack spreads and declined somewhat. Nonetheless, DINO's total return over 2022 was almost 60% versus the nearly 20% decline of the S&P 500 that year.

While share prices followed crack spread somewhat closely, trailing 12-month revenues lagged behind. In fact, revenues demonstrate a very clear dependence on the 12-month simple moving average of crack spread; these two metrics are in nearly perfect lockstep.

One might further observe DINO share prices peaked again in late 2023. An argument could be made that the market sentiment finally caught up with the company's lagging revenue expansion as it persisted well beyond crack spread highs. While this argument makes sense, direct causality is difficult to establish, especially as the company announced stock buybacks and a midstream acquisition around the same time in late 2023. These announcements probably boosted share prices, but they were likely only possible with funds generated by enduring revenue expansion.

Additional Tailwinds

DINO's most recent yearly report describes an improving operating environment for the company regarding both regulations and tax liabilities. The company was granted EPA waivers for fuel blending requirements that contributed $485 million to adjusted refinery gross margins over 2025. Given the company's total gross profit of just over $2.3B, this single regulatory relief added over 20% to gross profits.

Additionally, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act extended clean fuel tax incentives and certain depreciation allowances while also extending and enhancing beneficial provisions enacted under the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act. Taken together, these tax benefits contributed to the reduction of cash taxes paid to just $51M in 2025 vs $110M in 2024.

Without venturing a single unnecessary step into American politics, I expect friendly regulatory and tax policies to persist towards DINO and the energy sector as a whole for a few years.

Forward Earnings Estimates & Valuation

DINO shares have already more than doubled over the last year, but still look cheap based on YE26 projections. Seeking Alpha's consensus analyst estimates call for YE26 revenues to increase about 10% from $26.9B to $30.0B while normalized earnings are expected to increase about 140% from $2.58 to $6.11/share. Forward PE ratios based on consensus estimates of normalized forward earnings are tabulated below with reference to DINO and its US refining peers.

DINO is currently trading at 9.4 times forward earnings and appears cheap compared to its own five-year average of 20.9 times forward earnings. The company's ratio is also substantially lower than the average 11.6 forward PE of its peers. A fair value DINO share price estimate can be calculated based on peer average forward PE and consensus estimated earnings as follows:

Price/Share = Price/Earnings * EPS = 11.6 * $6.11 = $70.88/share

With shares currently trading at $57.51, over 20% upside is implied if we assume DINO's PE ratio will expand towards the average of its peers.

Investor Takeaways

With a fragile and incomplete ceasefire currently in place, the conflict centered around Iran is by no means resolved; I expect continuing uncertainty and volatility in global energy markets. DINO share price performance and revenues following the Russian invasion of Ukraine can provide some limited understanding of current market conditions. Shares are likely to spike and plummet alongside crude oil and fuel prices, while revenues will be determined by longer term trends around crack spread.

Most recently, diplomacy between the United States and Iran failed to reach any consensus. Iran continues to restrict traffic in the Strait of Hormuz while the United States plans a naval blockade of Iranian ports. In short, there is no resolution in sight and global energy markets will likely continue to be undersupplied.

Ongoing energy shortages are likely to provide further support for DINO's share price and future earnings. Although I do not expect an orderly or well-behaved market, I recommend those investors with appropriate risk tolerance buy DINO at current market prices.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook: U.S. Blockade On Hormuz Begins - Wall Street Withstands The Pressure
data.description:
By Elior Manier

This weekend marked the start of US-Iran negotiations, which, unsurprisingly, quickly turned sour.

A wave of US and Iranian envoys have come and gone from Islamabad, Pakistan, to maintain the diplomatic attempts – the first round of talks was unfortunately unfruitful.

Numerous disputes have halted discussions, with the US administration eager for an agreement but insisting on demands that the Islamic regime considers too harsh.

WTI crude surged nearly 10% to $104 at the Globex open, sparking pessimism in global stock markets.

The ceasefire is still ongoing, and as long as it continues, there is hope for a more peaceful path ahead.

President Trump recently decided to implement a blockade of blockades in the Strait of Hormuz, with Iran maintaining their leverage on the world's most important 10 km location.

Will this advance a deal? Uncertain, given ongoing threats to end the truce. Prediction markets now estimate the odds of a deal before April 30 at 20%, down from 35% on Friday.

Despite gapping lower at the open, US equities are remaining extremely resilient, with the S&P 500 holding right below unchanged and only the Dow Jones pulling back (~ -0.50%). The moves are still quite timid, definitely not pointing to a return of widespread panic.

While the Middle East situation remains in a status quo, traders are still maintaining optimistic views on the conflict's future. It is wise to keep a close eye on the latest developments to be ready to trade accordingly.

While doubts remain, it is always best to remain ready. Let's look at intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones Industrial Average, Nasdaq Composite, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

The market picture is quite cloudy, with many of the largest names hanging close to unchanged as participants are still uncertain on what to do next.

Producer manufacturing is a laggard, while tech (particularly electronics) is attempting a rebound.

Once again, sectorial trading seems quite compromised amid recent confusion; hence, it could be more strategic to either focus on individual stocks or index trading to avoid getting caught in crosswinds.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The Dow Jones Index gapped lower at the open, struggling the most out of the three main US benchmarks. However, sellers could not continue their short-lived dominance, and a rebound attempt is already on the way.

For the short term, look at 48,000 for immediate bull/bear guidance, as this will be a target for the ongoing rebound.

  • Rejecting it points to more downside ahead, pointing towards 47,000
  • Breaking back above 48,000 would look to retest the past week's highs (48,300) – Above this, the action turns long-term bullish.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Momentum Pivot at 48,000 (short-term bearish below)
  • March 4 resistance 48,250 - 48,300 (bull above)
  • Mini-resistance 48,700
  • Major Resistance – 49,000 to 49,200

Support Levels

  • Major Pivotal Support 47,400 to 47,600 (Session lows)
  • War Resistance now Key Support 47,000 +/- 100 Points (Bearish below)
  • March 8 War lows Resistance now Support 46,300
  • 45,700 to 45,900 August Support
  • January 2025 Highs 45,000 to 45,280

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq remains persistent within its 25,000 to 25,250 area, forming a short-term consolidation range.

The longer the action stays in that zone, the higher the chances of an upside breakout.

Nonetheless, this zone is a longer-run resistance; hence, any bearish outflow could see heavy momentum – keep track of sentiment for more precise execution.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Key Resistance 25,000 to 25,250 (top at 25,250!)
  • 25,400 to 25,500 Feb Range resistance
  • Major resistance 25,700 to 25,850

Support Levels

  • 24,750 to 24,900 Momentum Pivot
  • 24,450 to 24,550 Pivotal Support
  • Feb Range Support 24,150 to 24,200
  • Major 2026 Pivotal Support 23,800 to 24,000
  • August 2025 Support 23,500 to 23,650
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 2H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 is already running back towards its Friday highs as traders maintain further hopes for continued talks.

Breaking 6,840 puts the index on a bullish short-term outlook. Failing to breach this level maintains a more range-bound outlook ahead.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Early March Resistance 6,820 to 6,840 (testing)
  • Key Resistance Zone 6,880 to 6,900
  • Previous ATH Resistance 6,945 to 6,975

Support Levels

  • Major Momentum Pivot 6,750 to 6,770
  • 6,680 to 6,700 Pivotal Support (4H 200-period MA)
  • 6,580 to 6,610 Support
  • 4H 50-period MA 6,550
  • 6,490 to 6,520 October lows
  • 6,300 psychological level (War lows)

The narrative is still confusing, so keep track of WTI Crude and the latest headlines throughout the week to stay ahead of the game.

data.title: EIM: Discount Widens Back To A More Attractive Level
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Eaton Vance Municipal Bond Fund (EIM) has been under recent pressure once again as long-term rates start to rise and short-term rates are expected to remain elevated. As a closed-end fund that invests in municipal bonds, it is highly sensitive to interest rates, and that is compounded by its leverage. Given the outlook was the expectation for further rate cuts from the Fed, with those on hold due to rising oil prices likely to keep inflation sticky even longer, that can continue to keep its borrowing costs elevated.

However, some positive news is that the fund has also seen its discount widen since our last update. Given the added pressures on the fund, combined with that, it can make it a more interesting time to consider adding to this position than it was previously.

EIM Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.36
  • Discount/Premium: -6.70%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.16%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.17%
  • Leverage: 30.66%
  • Managed Assets: $787.28 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

EIM's investment objective is to “provide current income exempt from federal income tax.”

They intend to achieve this through:

... at least 80% of the Fund’s net assets will be invested in municipal obligations, the interest on which is exempt from federal income tax, including the alternative minimum tax (“AMT”), and that are rated A or better by Moody’s Investors Service, Inc. (“Moody’s”), S&P Global Ratings (“S&P”) or Fitch Ratings (“Fitch”). The foregoing 80% policy may not be changed without shareholder approval. Under normal market conditions, the Fund expects to be fully invested (at least 95% of its net assets) in accordance with its investment objective. The Fund may invest up to 20% of its net assets in municipal obligations rated BBB/Baa or below (or unrated obligations deemed by the Fund’s adviser, Eaton Vance Management (“Eaton Vance”), to be of equivalent quality), provided that not more than 15% of its net assets may be invested in municipal obligations rated below B (or unrated obligations deemed by Eaton Vance to be of equivalent quality) and may invest up to 20% of its net assets in bonds on which the interest is subject to the AMT. When a municipal obligation is split rated (meaning rated in different categories by Moody’s, S&P, or Fitch) the Fund will deem the higher rating to apply.

When including the fund's interest expenses, the total expense ratio comes up to 2.88%. Since the last annual report, financing costs would have come down even further, but we'll be discussing that further below. While leverage has the positive impact of potentially higher returns moving forward, it also comes with greater overall risk to the downside and increased volatility. That can lead to what would otherwise be a fairly conservative municipal bond portfolio, not being appropriate for every investor.

Discount Widens Driving Flat Results

The fund has delivered about flat total returns since our last update, but it actually would have been better in terms of its total NAV return results. The total NAV return results being that of its actual underlying portfolio.

This is because the fund was sporting around a 2% discount, a historically narrow level, before it has now widened back out. In our last update, we were also discussing the tender offer, which is likely the key catalyst keeping the discount at such a narrow level. In fact, the day we ended up covering the name, it was surging over 4%.

Even further, over the longer term, the fund's discount is incredibly narrow compared to its average level. That has made it relatively less appealing as a choice for putting capital to work today.

If I were to have to guess, I suspect we might see the price fall back down after Friday's unusual surge. Trading volume was a bit higher than average, but it wasn't anything too dramatic at 1.32x the average (~195k compared to ~148k daily).

Well, my guess proved to be right as the share price did fall quite dramatically shortly after. It was then that the latest pressures on this muni-focused CEF put added downward force on the fund's discount. Those pressures are coming from a rising 10 Year U.S. Treasury Rate on the back of the Iran war and rising oil prices. This has been enough to push it back toward its historical discount level—but not exactly over the level yet. Though more recently it briefly did breach the 9% level, thanks to the more significant volatility we've seen lately.

Given the share price pressures from both higher interest rates and discount widening, it makes it a more interesting choice for investors to consider adding at this time.

High Distribution From Munis, But Watching Coverage

The fund's current distribution rate works out to 6.16%, and a NAV rate of 5.75%. That's quite high for municipal bonds, and that can be attributed a bit to the leverage, as investors expect a higher rate when investing in a leveraged fund.

However, when looking at the fund's leverage, it is sensitive to short-term rates as its borrowing costs are through floating-rate notes. In our prior update, we were discussing the positive outlook on this front from further rate cuts. Since that write-up, we did get another 75 basis points of cuts, which should have helped to alleviate the borrowing costs further. However, the outlook for further rate cuts heading into 2026 has now been put on hold.

We noted previously that the fund was not covering its payout entirely from net investment income either. Meaning that they were choosing to pay an elevated rate, likely to keep interest in the fund. This isn't anything new to municipal bond CEFs, which most regularly overpay what they actually earn.

Diving into the latest UNII report from the Eaton Vance fixed-income CEFs, we can see that EIM's latest quarterly coverage sits at around 79%.

That's actually quite the improvement from the 70% we noted from the fund's semi-annual report, for the period ending March 31, 2025.

For the full fiscal year period ending September 30, 2025, we also saw distribution coverage sit similarly at around 70.5%. Therefore, we are seeing those next 75 basis points of cuts in late 2025 starting to kick in during the latest rolling three-month UNII report.

That's the positive, but we are now at a point where this will most likely not see any further added boosts higher. At least, not material boosts unless they start to turn over their portfolio to higher-yielding holdings. As they focus primarily on higher-quality municipal bonds, I wouldn't anticipate seeing any sort of dramatic shift unless they change the investment policy of the fund.

EIM's Portfolio

The fund's average effective duration comes in at 6.68 years, and its leverage-adjusted duration comes in at 9.65 years. Thus, highlighting just how interest rate sensitive the fund's underlying portfolio is. This is primarily driven by the high-quality exposure of muni funds and being able to get away with extended maturities. In this case, the average maturity comes in at 20.34 years, across its 175 portfolio holdings.

For the investors who have to worry about the alternative minimum tax, the fund lists that it has 9.63% that is exposed to AMT.

In looking at the top holdings, it isn't too heavily concentrated in any specific holding. The two largest holdings could be considered somewhat relatively overweight relative to the others that follow, but we quickly drop to around the 1% allocation range by the end of the top ten.

This is relatively more concentrated compared to its high-yield CEF peers out there, but given the higher quality and the significantly reduced expectations for defaults, this would appear fine. One of the reasons why high-yield funds hold hundreds and hundreds, or even into the thousands, of different holdings is precisely because of the higher risk for default. If they spread their portfolio more diversely, that limits the downside risks of only a handful of positions causing too much damage.

Conclusion

Thanks to the increased volatility lately, we've seen EIM's discount widen back out. There is a more pessimistic outlook with higher rates, and that comes in the form of higher short-term rates as well. Higher short-term rates due to the Fed likely holding rates steady in 2026 mean borrowing costs on the leverage will stay elevated. This was after the outlook was initially for continued cuts, which had seen NII coverage increase quite meaningfully over the last couple of years. That said, when the fund, or any investment, is under pressure, that is often the time to consider adding for long-term investors.

data.title: Tax-Loss Harvesting With Bonds Vs. Stocks: Different Rules, Same Goal
data.description:
By Paul Robertson, Gavin Romm, CFA and Mark Gleason, CFA

Investors shouldn’t overlook bonds as a key part of the tax management arsenal.

Tax management is about more than just deferring taxes to reduce this year’s bite. It’s also about managing where and how taxes show up over time. For high-net-worth investors with diversified portfolios, permanently reducing taxes versus deferring them may bolster long-term after-tax wealth. Many investors overlook a potent tax reduction tool: bonds.

The Mechanics of Tax-Loss Harvesting with Stocks

With tax-loss harvesting, realizing a loss on one stock can offset a realized gain elsewhere, reducing the current year’s capital gains expense.

Let’s say you previously bought 10 shares of ABC Corporation’s stock at $100 per share. Its cost basis is $1,000 (10 shares times $100). You sell the stock for $50 per share and reinvest the proceeds in a replacement stock; its cost basis would be $500 (the reinvested amount).

Your portfolio’s cost basis drops by $500, but its market value has already fallen. This increases the net unrealized gain, so future turnover will likely create a bigger realized gain or a smaller realized loss. You didn’t eliminate taxes, but you did alter when and how you’ll pay them.

Taxes Deferred Keep More After-Tax Dollars at Work

But the “kicking the can down the road” strategy of tax deferral can be effective, even if that can is bigger later on. With a reduced tax burden this year, you keep more after-tax dollars at work in your portfolio. If you invest smartly, you have the potential to boost your after-tax wealth by more than enough to pay the bigger bill later.

In truth, there’s really only one way to avoid having to pay more taxes in later years. That’s to never sell the replacement stock. If you die before selling it, the portfolio qualifies for a step-up in cost basis, eliminating the unrealized gain. But that “fix,” as we see it, restricts the ability of your investments to build the wealth you need to support your lifestyle.

Bonds’ Design Creates a Low Profile for Capital Gains and Losses

This dynamic raises a key question: is there a way to harvest losses without boosting future tax exposure? In our view, bonds can play a powerful and often overlooked role in this respect, making them a key part of the tax-loss harvesting tool kit in their own right (Display).

Much of it has to do with the way bonds work. When you sell a bond at a loss and reinvest the proceeds in another bond, that investment will mature at its face value, or par. Often, it does so without producing a taxable gain. The key is to buy the replacement bond generally at a price that’s above its par value and higher than the de minimis threshold.

Using this technique means that the difference between your portfolio’s market value and cost basis is not a reliable guide to your future capital gains. Of course, any bond can be sold before it matures at a price that creates a realized gain or loss. But bonds can also be held to maturity and—if they were bought above the de minimis threshold—don’t result in a capital gain or loss.

Bonds, particularly municipals, typically generate fewer capital gains over time, so investors can harvest losses today without significantly increasing future tax exposure. As a result, they may be especially effective for reducing or even fully offsetting smaller capital gains in a given year.

The Big Picture

As we’ve said before, investors should leave no stone unturned in boosting after-tax returns. In that effort, both stocks and bonds are useful in tax-loss harvesting. If you have only small capital gains to offset, it may make sense to start your tax-loss harvesting on the bond side of your portfolio.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams. The views should not be considered to be legal or tax advice. The tax rules are complicated, and their impact on a particular individual may differ depending on the individual’s specific circumstances. Views are subject to revision over time.
